content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 9,
1993
Honorable Gaddi H.
Vasquez
Supervisor, Third District
Robert E.
Thomas
Hall of Administration
10 Civic Center Plaza
Santa Ana,
CA
92701
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93--065
Dear Supervisor Vasquez:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice as a
member of the Orange County Board of Supervisors regarding your
responsibilities pursuant to the provisions of the Political
Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
QUESTIONS
1.
May you accept free air transportation and accommodations
from the American Jewish Congress for a trip to Israel to meet
with Israeli officials and confer on governing multi-ethnic
jurisdictions?
2.
What are your obligations with respect to the receipt of
free air transportation and accommodations from the American
Jewish Congress?
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A—93-065
Page
2
CONCLUSIONS
1.
Local elected officials are subject to a $1,000 limit on
the receipt of gifts and honoraria from any single source during a
calendar year.
However,
Section 89501 exempts gifts of actual
travel expenses and reasonable subsistence in connection with
attendance at any public or private conference,
convention,
meeting,
social event, meal,
or like gathering.
Thus,
you are not
prohibited from accepting the gift from the American Jewish
Congress.
2.
You are required to disclose the gift and disqualify
yourself from any decision which would have a foreseeable and
material financial effect on the American Jewish Congress during
the 12 months after receiving the gift.
FACTS
You have been invited by the United States Conference of
Mayors to accompany a delegation of elected officials traveling to
Israel in March.
The purpose of the trip is to meet with Israeli
officials to discuss the challenges related to governing multi
ethnic jurisdictions.
The program will last eight days.
The event is jointly sponsored by the American Jewish
Congress, the United States Conference of Mayors,
and the Hanns—
Seidel Foundation.
You stated that the American Jewish Congress
has offered to pay for your transportation and accommodations
while you are at the conference.
The American Jewish Congress is
a nonprofit organization formed pursuant to Internal Revenue Code
Section 501(c) (3).
ANALYSIS
The Act defines a “gift” in pertinent part,
as:
{A)ny payment to the extent that consideration
of equal or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of
anything of value unless the rebate or discount is
made in the regular course of business to members
of the public without regard to official status.
Any person, other than a defendant in a criminal
action,
who claims that a payment is not a gift by
reason of receipt of consideration has the burden
of proving that the consideration received is of
equal or greater value.
Section 82028(a).


Our File No.
A—93-065
Page
3
Thus,
absent some exception,
any person who provides
transportation and accommodations to you free of charge has
provided a gift to you.
Section 82028 does not provide an
exception for gifts of travel and per diem from bona fide
charitable, educational or academic organizations.
2
(Quan Advice
Letter,
No.
A—89—182a..)
Section 89501 provides:
No local elected officeholder shall accept any
honoraria for any speech given, article published,
or attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal,
or like
gathering,
or any gifts,
from any single source,
which is in excess of one thousand dollars
($1,000),
in any calendar year,
except
reimbursement for actual travel expenses and
reasonable subsistence in connection therewith.
Section 89501.
However,
Section 89501 exempts gifts of actual travel
expenses and reasonable subsistence in connection with attendance
at any public or private conference, convention, meeting, social
event, meal,
or like gathering,
from the gift limit of Section
89501.
Thus, actual transportation and reasonable subsistence
would not be subject to the limits of Section 8950l.
Other Responsibilities
While Section 89501 exempts the payments for trave1 and
accommodations from the gift limits,
such payments continue to be
considered gifts under the Act.
You are required to disclose the
gift (Section 87207; Regulation 18950.2(b))
and disqualify
yourself from any decision which would have a foreseeable and
material financial effect on the American Jewish Congress during
2
Section 82030 does provide an exception for travel expenses and
per diem received from a bona fide educational,
academic,
or
charitable organization if the payment was received in exchange
for full and adequate consideration.
However, the burden is on
the official to demonstrate that he or she provided consideration
of equal or greater value.
You have submitted no facts to
indicate that you will be providing full and adequate
consideration to the American Jewish Congress for the
transportation to and lodging in Israel.
Of course,
any other payments would be a gift subject to the
limits of Section 89501.


Our File No.
A—93-065
Page
4
the
12 months after receiving the gift.
We have enclosed some
general conflict of interest material for your information.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—59Ol.
Sincerely
Jeff Marschner
General Counsel
By:
‘John W. Wallace
Counsel,
Legal Division
‘
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of lOc per page.
